stories.headline: DIA Braces for Spring Break Surge as Government Shutdown Leaves TSA Officers Unpaid
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport is preparing for more than 1.3 million travelers to pass through security checkpoints between Wednesday and March 29, even as a partial government shutdown has left Transportation Security Administration officers working without full paychecks. TSA disruptions hit hard last weekend at airports in Atlanta, Houston and New Orleans, forcing travelers to wait up to three hours, raising concerns about similar delays at DIA.

Wait times at DIA remained mostly under eight minutes as of Tuesday, but officials warned conditions could change quickly, particularly during peak travel windows of 3 to 4:30 a.m., 8 to 10 a.m., and 3 to 5 p.m. Denver officials advised travelers to check real-time security wait times at FlyDenver.com/security and to arrive inside the airport at least two hours before boarding.

The Department of Homeland Security shutdown began Feb. 14 over a dispute regarding restrictions on immigration operations. TSA officers are set to miss their first full paychecks this weekend, mirroring conditions experienced during a 43-day shutdown last year.

U.S. Rep. Brittany Pettersen called Tuesday on Congress to pass legislation ensuring TSA officers are paid during future government shutdowns. DIA's security infrastructure is undergoing a $2.1 billion overhaul scheduled for completion next year.

stories.headline: Semi With Failed Brakes Crushes Jeep Against Littleton Gas Station, One Seriously Injured
stories.summary:
A semitrailer with failed brakes crushed a Jeep Cherokee against a QuikTrip gas station at 2338 W. Belleview Ave. in Littleton on Tuesday afternoon, leaving one person in serious but stable condition. South Metro Fire Rescue crews responded just after 1:30 p.m. and worked alongside West Metro Fire Rescue technical rescue teams to stabilize the damaged building.

The driver of the CDS Transport semi said his brakes failed while traveling south on Santa Fe Drive. After exiting onto Belleview Avenue, the truck crossed a median before slamming into the gas station. The semi driver was treated for minor injuries and declined hospital transport.

Littleton Police spokeswoman Sheera Poelman said officials were surprised the Jeep's occupant survived, given the extent of the damage visible in photos posted by South Metro Fire Rescue showing the vehicle fully crumpled between the truck and the building. The injured person is expected to recover.

The cause of the brake failure is under investigation. The crash prompted a significant emergency response and raised questions about vehicle maintenance for commercial carriers operating on the busy Santa Fe Drive corridor.

stories.headline: Aurora Mayor Opposes Settlement in First Amendment Lawsuit, Calls It 'Unfortunate'
stories.summary:
Aurora Mayor Mike Coffman publicly denounced a settlement reached between the City of Aurora and activist MiDian Holmes at a City Council meeting Monday night, calling the outcome "unfortunate" and criticizing the speech of those who had sued the city. The settlement, announced in early March, resolves a lawsuit Holmes filed in June 2025 alleging that council decisions to curtail public comment were unconstitutional.

Under the settlement terms, the Aurora City Council must hold in-person meetings with a public comment period for the next three years, allowing up to one hour of comment with a maximum of three minutes per speaker. The city is also required to pay $75,000 in attorneys' fees, and officials must work with the Ad Hoc Rules Committee to develop conduct rules with meaningful public input.

Coffman opened the public comment period with a statement opposing the settlement, saying he found certain speakers' conduct offensive. Holmes responded from the podium, accusing the mayor of guiding the city into repeated litigation. "When this council decided to remove public comment and violate the First Amendment, you should've been grappling with that harder than we did," Holmes said.

Holmes and supporters of Kilyn Lewis, a person whose interactions with Aurora Police sparked the broader controversy, have attended nearly every council meeting for two years. The lawsuit stemmed from council votes to hold virtual meetings and eliminate public comment pending a judicial decision in the Lewis family's separate case.

stories.headline: Judge's Ruling Against Colorado Forced Prison Labor Raises Broader Questions About Operations
stories.summary:
Viewers and advocacy groups are pressing for answers following a Denver District Court ruling last month that found forced labor conditions exist in Colorado state prisons. Judge Sarah Wallace agreed with plaintiffs from the class action lawsuit that prisoners were threatened with segregation and isolation if they refused to work, and ordered the Colorado Department of Corrections to stop such practices.

Among the questions emerging from the public: how CDOC will staff essential operations such as cooking and cleaning if prisoners opt out of work, and whether past unpaid labor could trigger compensation claims. Michael Gibson-Light of End Slavery Colorado, one of the groups behind the lawsuit, said the practical effects will take time to become clear, but emphasized the ruling's significance in abolishing coerced labor even for incarcerated individuals.

CDOC has not responded to multiple inquiries from Denver7 regarding staffing impacts or back pay questions. Gov. Jared Polis' office reiterated a February statement saying the governor opposes slavery and forced servitude and that the administration was reviewing the order to determine next steps. The state faces a Friday deadline to file an appeal.

The ruling is part of a broader national conversation about prison labor practices and the limits of the Thirteenth Amendment's exemption for those convicted of crimes. Colorado voters approved a constitutional amendment in 2018 banning slavery and involuntary servitude in state prisons, forming part of the legal basis for the lawsuit.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Advances Municipal Court Transparency Bill, Kills Records Delay Measure
stories.summary:
The Colorado House voted 43-19 to advance House Bill 1134, which would prohibit municipal courts from jailing people unless the courts maintain records of their proceedings and hold hearings open to the public. The bill, sponsored by Reps. Javier Mabry and Elizabeth Velasco, was prompted in part by Denver Post reporting that found many municipal courts were not livestreaming hearings despite a 2023 law requiring them to do so.

Separately, a bipartisan Senate committee voted to kill Senate Bill 107, which would have extended the state's public records response window from three to five business days, and from 10 to 15 working days for requests requiring extensions. Senators Katie Wallace, Tom Sullivan and Lynda Zamora Wilson voted against the measure, which Gov. Polis had vetoed in similar form last year.

House Bill 1134 still requires Senate passage before it can become law. Supporters say the bill addresses a documented accountability gap in Colorado's court system, where some judges had openly refused to comply with livestreaming requirements.

Opponents of Senate Bill 107 argued the existing three-day response standard is appropriate given that most government records are stored electronically and can be retrieved quickly and at minimal cost. The bill's sponsors, Sens. Cathy Kipp and Janice Rice, were credited with good intentions, but critics said the final version would have undermined Colorado's open-records framework.

stories.headline: Aurora Council Unanimously Adopts Tobacco and Psychoactive Substance Retail License
stories.summary:
Aurora City Council gave final unanimous approval Monday to an ordinance creating a new retail license for businesses that sell tobacco, hemp, kratom, vaping products and other age-restricted psychoactive substances. The license costs $500 annually and is designed to fund twice-yearly compliance checks aimed at preventing youth access to such products.

The ordinance requires licensed retailers to be at least 1,500 feet from schools and 500 feet from similar businesses. Vape stores must be 2,000 feet apart, and hookah lounges must maintain a two-mile buffer from one another. The roughly 340 existing tobacco retailers in Aurora are expected to generate approximately $170,000 annually through license fees, plus an estimated $30,000 from fines.

Penalties escalate from a $1,000 fine for a first violation to license revocation on a fourth offense within 36 months. Existing businesses are grandfathered from distance restrictions, and the ordinance does not impose a flavor ban despite earlier discussions. The Adams County Board of Health, which supported the ordinance, cited tobacco as the leading cause of preventable death in Colorado.

The measure was brought forward by Councilmembers Ruben Medina and Stephanie Hancock after months of community input, including testimony from high school students. A companion ordinance establishing an annual reporting requirement for the license's effectiveness was moved to the next council meeting for a separate vote.

stories.headline: SpaceX Veteran's Space Company Opens 20,000-Square-Foot Manufacturing Facility Near Boulder
stories.summary:
Impulse Space, a California-based in-space propulsion company founded by a former SpaceX executive, has opened a new 20,000-square-foot manufacturing facility near Boulder to produce navigation and control systems for its Mira and Helios spacecraft. The company, founded in 2021, raised $300 million last year and has been growing its Colorado workforce for three years.

The new facility will focus on propulsion and mobility systems designed to move satellites and spacecraft efficiently once they reach orbit, a challenge the company says is distinct from the launch phase. President and COO Eric Romo said the Colorado team is doing foundational work for what Impulse describes as the in-space mobility infrastructure of the future.

Impulse Space has a growing portfolio of contracts with both private and government customers and said it is actively hiring in Colorado. The state's deep aerospace talent pool, anchored by decades of defense and space industry presence along the Front Range, was cited as a key factor in the expansion.

The opening adds to a string of aerospace expansions in the Boulder and Denver metro corridor, reinforcing Colorado's position as a hub for the commercial space sector.

stories.headline: Denver Tech Center Coworking-Golf Concept Expands; Capital Grille Opens Second Colorado Location
stories.summary:
The Clubhouse Work & Golf in the Denver Tech Center has amended its lease at the Triad building at 5680 Greenwood Plaza Blvd. to add 30 private offices and two Trackman iO golf simulators, along with two conference rooms, after reaching full capacity since opening over a year ago. The DTC expansion is slated to open this fall.

The company is also opening a second location this summer near Cherry Creek at 720 S. Colorado Blvd., which will feature 65 private offices, coworking space, meeting rooms and five golf simulators across two floors. Founder Josh Kaplan said demand from professionals seeking a workspace centered around golf networking drove both expansions.

In a separate development, The Capital Grille is opening its second Colorado location Sunday at 9067 Westview Road in Lone Tree, near Park Meadows Retail Resort, replacing a former Red Lobster. Reservations are now live for the fine-dining steakhouse chain.

The cluster of upscale business and dining openings in the south metro reflects continued consumer and commercial investment in the Denver Tech Center and Lone Tree corridors.

stories.headline: I-25 to Close Overnight Thursday Near Broomfield for Pedestrian Bridge Deck Work
stories.summary:
Interstate 25 will close in both directions near the Colorado 7 interchange north of metro Denver from 10 p.m. Thursday to 5 a.m. Friday as crews lay the bridge deck for a new pedestrian crossing over the highway. Southbound drivers will be detoured via exit 229 for Colorado 7 before immediately rejoining I-25, with the closure running from 10 p.m. to 4 a.m. Northbound drivers face a similar detour from 11 p.m. to 5 a.m.

The Colorado Department of Transportation said the work is part of the I-25 and Colorado 7 Interim Mobility Hub project, which will include a new Park-and-Ride lot, Bustang bus ramps, a water quality pond and the pedestrian bridge. Girders for the bridge were installed about a month ago.

The project sits along the boundary of Broomfield and Adams counties and is designed to improve regional transit connections and reduce travel times for commuters using the US 36 and I-25 corridor. The full project is slated for completion in fall 2026.

CDOT advised drivers to expect delays and use alternate routes during the overnight closure windows.

stories.headline: Cardi B Takes Oxygen Break Backstage at Sold-Out Denver Ball Arena Concert
stories.summary:
Rapper Cardi B performed for a sold-out crowd at Ball Arena in Denver on Monday night but required supplemental oxygen between songs during backstage costume changes. An Instagram Story posted by the artist showed her breathing from an oxygen tank while being helped into a red beaded corset. "Denver is different," she said, referencing the city's mile-high altitude.

Cardi B is on a U.S. tour supporting her album "Am I the Drama?", released last September, with the tour having begun in February just months after the birth of her fourth child in November. Her next scheduled show is Thursday in Minneapolis.

The rapper joins a long list of performers who have noted the physical effects of performing at 5,280 feet elevation. Lady Gaga experienced altitude sickness during a Denver concert in 2014, and many touring acts have commented on breathlessness during Colorado shows.

Denver's altitude presents a recurring challenge for visiting performers, particularly those dancing or moving vigorously on stage, and venues have begun keeping oxygen on hand as a standard backstage amenity.

stories.headline: Broncos Safety P.J. Locke Signs One-Year Deal With Cowboys, Ending Six-Year Denver Tenure
stories.summary:
Former Denver Broncos safety P.J. Locke has agreed to a one-year contract with the Dallas Cowboys, ending a six-year run with the team that began when he signed as an undrafted free agent in 2020. A source confirmed the deal to The Denver Post on Tuesday. Locke issued a farewell statement on social media, saying Denver felt like home for his family.

Locke worked his way from a special-teams contributor to a full-time starter at safety, recording 74 tackles and three tackles for loss in 15 starts in 2024. His role diminished after Denver signed Talanoa Hufanga in the offseason, but he made five starts in 2025 after Brandon Jones was lost for the season to a pectoral injury. He also recovered from an offseason spinal-fusion procedure — a surgery rarely survived professionally — to return to play virtually unimpaired.

Locke had a standout performance in Denver's AFC divisional-round playoff win over the Buffalo Bills this past January, recording nine tackles, a forced fumble and an interception. He will now compete for a larger role in Dallas under defensive coordinator Christian Parker, who coached him as the Broncos' defensive backs coach from 2021 to 2023, and returns to his home state of Texas.

Several NFL sources told The Post the Broncos will look to add safety depth in free agency or the draft to backfill Locke's departure. Denver has been increasingly connected to Toledo safety Emmanuel McNeil-Warren with the 30th overall pick in April's NFL Draft.

stories.headline: Avalanche Host Oilers in MacKinnon-McDavid Showdown as Colorado Leads Central Division
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche (43-10-9) host the Edmonton Oilers (31-25-8) on Wednesday night at Ball Arena in a nationally televised matchup on TNT, with puck drop set for 8:20 p.m. MT. The game features a marquee headlining matchup between Avalanche captain Nathan MacKinnon and Oilers star Connor McDavid, two of the NHL's top players.

Colorado enters the game as the top team in the Central Division after a strong stretch that included recent wins over Dallas in a shootout on the night of the Nazem Kadri trade and a sweep of the weekend Wild series. Kadri is slated to skate on the top line alongside MacKinnon and Martin Necas in Wednesday's projected lineup.

The Avalanche are playing without captain Gabe Landeskog, who is listed as week-to-week with a lower-body injury. Despite the absence, Colorado's depth has allowed the team to maintain its division lead with one of the best records in the Western Conference.

The Oilers, sitting at 31-25-8, are fighting for a playoff position and will look to McDavid to generate offense against a Colorado defensive group anchored by Cale Makar and Devon Toews on the blue line. Mackenzie Blackwood is expected to start in goal for Colorado.

joke.joke: I paid for college and all I got was older.

topStory.headline: U.S.-Israel War With Iran Enters 12th Day as Oil Prices Surge, Recession Risk Grows
topStory.summary:
The United States and Israel's joint military campaign against Iran has reached its 12th day, with missiles, drones and airstrikes continuing to strike targets across the region. The conflict, which escalated sharply following joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on Feb. 28, has killed more than 1,000 people and triggered the largest oil supply disruption in history, according to economists. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has sent global energy markets into turmoil and raised alarms about the stability of the broader world economy.

U.S. oil prices briefly reached $119 a barrel early Monday, and average gasoline prices in the United States climbed to $3.48 per gallon, up 50 cents from $2.98 before the war began. Economists warn that if oil reaches $125 a barrel and gas hits $4.25 a gallon, the U.S. economy could tip into recession. Prediction market Kalshi placed the odds of a recession this year as high as 35% on Monday, up from roughly 20% in early February. University of Michigan economics professor Justin Wolfers said the economy has been 'on the precipice of recession for quite some time' and that an oil shock could be sufficient to push it over.

In Britain, Prime Minister Keir Starmer has navigated pressure from U.S. President Donald Trump to offer unconditional support for the Iran strikes. Starmer allowed the use of British bases for what the government characterized as defensive operations but stopped short of deeper military involvement, citing international law and British national interests. Labour MP Matt Western, writing in a personal capacity, drew comparisons to Harold Wilson's refusal to commit British troops to Vietnam and noted that the U.S.-UK relationship has seen significant divergence on foreign policy multiple times since World War II, including during the Suez crisis and the Falklands conflict.

Analysts warn the conflict carries risks of stagflation — a combination of rising inflation and slowing economic growth — that could prove particularly damaging given already fragile conditions in major economies. The U.S. economy added only 116,000 jobs in all of 2025, the lowest total outside a recession since 2002, leaving it more vulnerable to an oil shock than during the 2022 price spike that followed Russia's invasion of Ukraine. The Federal Reserve could face pressure to raise interest rates even as growth slows, a scenario that economists say would compound financial strain on households and businesses alike. How long the Strait of Hormuz remains closed is considered the central variable in determining whether the economic disruption deepens into a prolonged crisis.
